page: Amazon Web Services r4.16xlarge is a memory-optimized fourth-generation instance powered by the Xen hypervisor. It features 64 virtual CPUs mapped to 32 physical cores of an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor running at 2.3 GHz. The architecture is x86_64 with dedicated CPU allocation. It is equipped with 488.0 GB of system memory, yielding approximately 7.62 GB of RAM per physical core. This instance does not include local storage or GPU accelerators, relying entirely on network-attached storage and network-based resources. Network connectivity is rated at a baseline speed of 25 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks reveal a clear tradeoff between single-core and multi-core capabilities. Single-core CPU performance, measured via stress-ng and Geekbench, falls into the bottom 10% tier, and single-threaded compression tasks are similarly weak. However, multi-core CPU performance and multi-threaded decompression scale to average levels within the middle 50% of tested systems. Memory bandwidth tests for both small cached blocks and large uncached blocks also register in the average tier, with large block read speeds reaching 106.11 GB/sec and write speeds at 44.59 GB/sec.

From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the r4.16xlarge offers a high density of memory relative to its compute resources, making it suitable for memory-bound workloads that do not require high single-thread execution speeds. Database operations, Redis caching (achieving 35.7 million SET operations per second), static web serving, and multi-threaded data processing represent the primary workload fits. It can also handle moderate LLM inference tasks using smaller models, though text generation speeds degrade on larger models.
description: Amazon Web Services r4.16xlarge is a memory-optimized instance designed for memory-intensive workloads. It features 64 vCPUs from an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor running at 2.3 GHz, paired with 488.0 GB of RAM and a 25 Gbps baseline network interface. The instance does not include local storage or GPU hardware. Benchmark data indicates a significant performance tradeoff, with single-core CPU execution and single-threaded compression scoring in the bottom 10% to 25% tiers, while multi-core performance, memory bandwidth, and multi-threaded operations perform at average levels. With 7.62 GB of memory per core, this instance is qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-heavy applications rather than compute-bound tasks. It is suited for database operations, Redis caching, static web serving, and multi-threaded data processing.
og_description: Amazon Web Services r4.16xlarge is a memory-optimized instance featuring 64 vCPUs, 488 GB RAM, and 25 Gbps network bandwidth, delivering average multi-core performance with lower single-core efficiency.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services r4.16xlarge is a memory-optimized instance with 64 vCPUs, 488 GB RAM, and 25 Gbps network speed, suited for database workloads.
tagline: A memory-optimized instance featuring 488 GB of RAM and 64 vCPUs, balanced for memory-intensive multi-threaded workloads and database operations.
bullet_points: Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor with 64 vCPUs and 32 physical cores
488.0 GB of system memory optimized for memory-bound applications
25 Gbps baseline network bandwidth with no local storage or GPU
Average multi-core and memory bandwidth benchmark performance
Suited for database operations, Redis caching, and static web serving
